data.title: AMLP: Understanding The Structure And Suitability Of This Income ETF
data.description:
The Alerian MLP ETF (NYSEARCA:AMLP) is an exchange-traded index fund offered by SS&C ALPS Advisors that is designed to track the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. The Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index is a specialized index that tracks energy infrastructure companies that are structured as master limited partnerships. There are relatively few companies that fall into this category, and as such, the index only includes thirteen companies. This low number of constituent companies is a result of two crises that struck the energy industry during the decade following the 2009 Great Recession. The first of these crises was a 2014 collapse in oil prices. The second crisis was the COVID-19 pandemic, which also caused a temporary collapse in energy prices. During these two events, many master limited partnerships found themselves having difficulty obtaining capital and they ended up restricting their businesses as corporations, in the case of Kinder Morgan (KMI) and Targa Resources (TRGP), or merging with larger midstream companies, as was the case with Crestwood Equity Partners and Energy Transfer (ET) in 2023. These restructuring and acquisition activities have reduced the number of companies that are eligible for inclusion in the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index over the years, and as mentioned, there are now only thirteen.

There is another index from Alerian that is very similar to the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. This other index is called the “Alerian MLP Index,” and it tracks seventeen companies. Despite the name of the fund suggesting that it might track this other index, that is not the case. The website for the Alerian MLP ETF specifically states (my emphasis):

The Alerian MLP ETF seeks investment results that correspond (before fees and expenses) generally to the price and yield performance of its underlying index, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index(AMZI).

The website for the Alerian MLP ETF also includes this handy description of the fund:

Once again, this confirms that the Alerian MLP ETF is tracking the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index and not the Alerian MLP Index.

The Alerian MLP Index includes four more companies than the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, but the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index still contains about 95% of the Alerian MLP Index. The difference between the two comes from their methodology. Any company meeting the following criteria is included in the Alerian MLP Index:

  • Structured as a master limited partnership,
  • Derives more than 50% of its trailing twelve-month cash flow from midstream energy operations,
  • Has a market capitalization of at least $75 million.

On the other hand, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index has the following criteria for inclusion:

  • Structured as a master limited partnership,
  • Derives more than 50% of its cash flow from midstream energy operations,
  • Had a median daily trading volume of at least $2 million over the past six months,
  • Paid a distribution for the past two quarters.

The last two bullet points here are the key differences. Rather than simply requiring a minimum market capitalization, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index has minimum daily trading and distribution requirements that eliminate companies that are illiquid or financially weak. This should make the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index a better pick for most investors. After all, illiquid or financially weak companies are generally only suitable investments for investors with very high levels of risk tolerance and large enough portfolios not to be bothered by losses or an inability to access their money on demand. This does not describe most retirees or near-retirees looking to use their portfolios to support their needs.

Important Characteristics Of The Alerian MLP ETF

The Alerian MLP ETF is a fairly popular index fund for investors who are seeking a very high level of income from the assets in their portfolios. A big reason for this is that, historically, companies that are structured as master limited partnerships are among the highest-yielding companies in the market. For example, let us look at the yields of the largest holdings of the Alerian MLP ETF:

Company

% of Fund Assets

Market Yield

MPLX LP (MPLX)

12.46%

7.68%

Sunoco LP (SUN)

12.27%

6.68%

Enterprise Products Partners LP (EPD)

11.91%

7.15%

Western Midstream Partners LP (WES)

11.81%

10.21%

Energy Transfer LP (ET)

11.41%

8.29%

Plains All American Pipeline LP (PAA)

11.05%

9.37%

Hess Midstream LP (HESM)

9.54%

7.62%

Cheniere Energy Partners LP (CQP)

5.27%

5.50%

USA Compression Partners LP (USAC)

3.66%

9.07%

Genesis Energy LP (GEL)

3.29%

4.72%

Suburban Propane Partners LP (SPH)

2.79%

6.50%

Global Partners LP (GLP)

2.78%

5.90%

Delek Logistics Partners LP (DKL)

1.65%

11.42%

While the exact yields of these companies will vary based on market performance, we can expect that they will almost always be among the highest-yielding companies in the market. In fact, most master limited partnerships have historically had higher yields than junk bonds, which alone would make them quite attractive to most income investors. However, we should keep in mind that all of the securities held by the Alerian MLP ETF are common equities as opposed to fixed-income securities, and these two asset classes will go through economic cycles very differently. Equities generally have substantially higher upside potential than any fixed-income securities. Over the five-year period that ended on May 7, 2025, the Alerian MLP ETF has substantially outperformed the domestic junk bond index (JNK):

While this will not always be the case, it does certainly show that the Alerian MLP ETF could provide a very attractive alternative to fixed-income securities for anyone who is seeking income and the preservation of purchasing power over time. After all, historically equities have been better than debt at preserving the purchasing power of wealth in the face of inflation. Thus, a high-yielding equity fund such as the Alerian MLP ETF could offer a worthy investment proposition for retirees looking for both a high level of income and the long-term preservation of purchasing power that is needed over the term of a lengthy retirement.

As the companies that comprise the Alerian MLP ETF have very high yields, we might expect the fund to have one too. This is the case as the fund yields 8.07% as of the time of writing. Historically, it usually trades with a distribution yield of around 7% to 9%, depending on the market level of the index. Thus, we can see that it is a bit better than most fixed-income index exchange-traded funds in terms of yield during most market conditions.

One of the big problems with the Alerian MLP ETF, unfortunately, is that it has a fairly large tracking error. According to Investopedia:

Tracking error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark.

In the case of an exchange-traded passive index fund like the Alerian MLP ETF, this refers specifically to how closely it tracks the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. In this case, the fund is not even close to the index. The fund’s website provides this chart of its annualized returns over various periods compared to those of the index that it tracks:

Please remember that in this case, the fund is tracking the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, so its performance against that index is more important than its performance against the Alerian MLP Index. We can immediately see noticeable differences between the Alerian MLP ETF and its benchmark in every single period, with most of them showing at least a few percentage points of difference. In fact, the Alerian MLP ETF has delivered a 5.28% total annualized return on net asset value since its inception date of August 25, 2010. The Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, on the other hand, has delivered a 7.84% total annualized return over the same period. That is a pretty big difference, as most index exchange-traded funds are able to get somewhat closer to matching their indices.

The biggest reason for this is that the Alerian MLP ETF has more than 25% of its assets invested in master limited partnerships, so tax laws require that it be structured as a C-corporation rather than as a pass-through entity like most funds. This means that the Alerian MLP ETF suffers a drag on its performance due to taxes. As Yahoo! Finance explains:

AMLP accrues deferred tax liability for its future tax liability on the capital appreciation of its investments, and the distributions it receives on equity securities of MLPs are considered a return of capital. The deferred income tax expense (or benefit) represents an estimate of AMLPs potential tax expense (or benefit) if it were to recognize the unrealized gains (or losses) in the portfolio. All realized and unrealized gains (or losses) on investments and expenses may vary greatly from year to year and from day to day, depending on the nature of the fund’s investments, the performance of those investments, and general market conditions.

The majority of exchange-traded funds simply pass the tax liabilities on to their investors and so do not have to pay taxes on a fund level. This allows them to more closely track their indices as they do not have the performance drag that comes from taxes. Unfortunately, tax laws prevent funds that have a significant proportion of their assets invested in master limited partnerships from doing this, and this is one reason why exchange-traded notes are usually a better option for investors who are looking to track master limited partnership indices. Such notes are much rarer today than they used to be, though, which is at least partially due to the two industry crises that were discussed in the introduction.

The fact that the Alerian MLP ETF is structured as a C-corporation does allow it to be used by retirement investors, which could be a benefit. In some cases, it can create tax problems if an investor puts a master limited partnership in a tax-advantaged retirement account such as an individual retirement account. As Fidelity Investments explains, master limited partnerships can cause a retirement account to generate current-year taxable income in some situations. Basically, an investor would have to pay taxes even though the distributions paid out by the master limited partnership were received in the retirement account and might not be withdrawn for many years in the future. This is different from dividends received by a corporation, which do not generate current-year taxation if the common stock is held in a retirement account. The Alerian MLP ETF, since it is structured as a corporation, also does not have this problem. The taxes are handled on a fund level, so investors holding shares of the fund in a retirement account will not have to pay current-year taxes on the fund’s distributions. The tax rules for this fund work exactly the same as any other corporation. Thus, the fund provides a way for investors to include master limited partnerships in their retirement accounts without tax complications.

Partially due to the fund itself being subject to taxes, the Alerian MLP ETF has a higher expense ratio than most passive index funds. The website states that its expense ratio is 0.85%:

Let us compare this to a few other popular passive exchange-traded index funds:

Fund Name

Expense Ratio

Alerian MLP ETF

0.85%

SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY)

0.0945%

Invesco QQQ ETF (QQQ)

0.2%

iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM)

0.19%

(all figures from the websites for the respective funds)

As we can see, the Alerian MLP ETF has a substantially higher expense ratio than other passive exchange-traded funds. As expenses are a drag on the fund’s ability to track the index, this is a contributing factor to its underperformance compared to the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index.

About Midstream Energy Companies

All of the companies that are included in the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, and by extension in the Alerian MLP ETF, are midstream energy companies. Midstream companies typically begin handling the hydrocarbon resources after they have been removed from the ground and frequently stop handling them once they reach the refinery. There may be some exceptions to this, such as refined products pipeline operators usually being considered midstream firms, even though refineries are downstream companies, but for the most part this definition suffices. Midstream companies generally engage in activities such as transporting, storing, and marketing oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids. Many midstream companies also engage in the processing of natural gas.

One of the defining characteristics of midstream companies is that they tend to conduct business under long-term contracts that provide them with very stable cash flow. For example, here is MPLX’s operating cash flow for each of the years over the 2015 to 2024 period:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

As we can see, the company’s operating cash flow never experienced a decline during that entire ten-year period. This is in spite of the fact that crude oil prices were all over the place during that period. In fact, even the 2020 COVID-19 oil price collapse did not cause its cash flow to decline. This shows the strength of the business models used by most of the companies that are included in the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. After all, as we saw earlier, one of the requirements for inclusion in this index is that the majority of the company’s cash flow comes from midstream operations. For nearly all of the companies in the index, that figure is far more than just a 50% majority.

The general cash flow stability of most midstream companies is nice for income investors because it provides a great deal of support for the distributions that they pay out. As we saw earlier, most midstream master limited partnerships have among the highest yields in the industry, and as such, they tend to attract investors who are seeking income. The last thing that most income-seekers want is for a company to cut its distribution, so the generally stable cash flows should provide some confidence that the company will be able to financially sustain its distributions.

At this point, there may be some readers who point out that some master limited partnerships have cut in the past during times of stress despite their stable cash flows. For example, Energy Transfer cut its formerly reliable distribution during the fourth quarter of 2020 (the announcement was made in October 2020, although it was officially the third-quarter 2020 distribution that was the first one at the lower level):

However, this was not actually due to a substantial decline in the company’s cash flow. The November 2020 distribution was 50% lower than the previous one, which was paid in August of 2020). It kept the distribution at that level until early 2022, when the company started raising it again. Energy Transfer’s operating cash flow did not decline by 50% in 2020 compared to 2019 levels. It also did not suffer from a lower operating cash flow in 2021 compared to previous years:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

The company’s operating cash flow for the full-year 2020 period came in at $7.3610 billion, which was only an 8.63% decline over the full-year 2019 level. Furthermore, the company’s operating cash flow for the full-year 2021 period came in stronger than the full-year 2019 level. Clearly, Energy Transfer did not cut its distribution because of financial problems. Rather, the company appears to have cut its distribution in order to use the money that would have been used for the distribution to reduce its debt. Thus, this does not disprove the statement that the midstream master limited partnerships comprising the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index typically have stable cash flows. However, this does illustrate a risk that investors may face if another energy price collapse occurs in the future. Unlike real estate investment trusts, master limited partnerships are not required to distribute a specific proportion of their earnings or cash flow to investors under U.S. tax laws. Some of them do have this requirement in their partnership agreements, but the fact that tax laws do not require the company to make distributions does allow them to cut their distributions even when their cash flow does not decrease. Thus, despite the generally stable cash flow, there is always the possibility that they will cut their distributions for whatever reason that management chooses. It is rare for this to occur, though, due to the fact that a distribution cut is taken as a bad sign by the market and usually causes the unit price of a given master limited partnership to decrease. During periods of time in which traditional energy companies are struggling to obtain capital in the markets, there could be a number of master limited partnerships cutting their distributions all at once. This would probably force the Alerian MLP ETF to also cut its distribution to its shareholders and, by extension, reduce the income of anyone holding it. This is a risk that investors should keep in mind when owning this exchange-traded fund.

How Investments Are Chosen For The Alerian MLP ETF

As stated earlier in the article, the Alerian MLP ETF is a passively-managed exchange-traded index fund that merely tracks the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. As such, its strategy is simply to buy the master limited partnerships that comprise the index in the same proportions as the index. As is the case with all exchange-traded funds, the Alerian MLP ETF is constantly issuing and destroying shares in response to investor demands. This is how it is able to keep its share price relatively consistent with its net asset value per share.

Basically, the way that this works is that an “Authorized Participant” is able to purchase all of the securities that comprise the fund’s portfolio in equal proportion to what the fund itself holds, and then exchange them for newly-created shares of the fund directly from the fund sponsor. It is also able to do the reverse of this (purchasing shares of the fund on the open market and exchanging them with the fund’s sponsor in exchange for the underlying securities from the fund). The fund’s shares that the Authorized Participant provides are cancelled whenever this occurs. The Authorized Participant is essentially arbitraging the fund’s shares and the underlying securities anytime that there is a significant difference between the fund’s market price and its net asset value. In so doing, any difference between the fund’s shares and its net asset value is kept minimal because Authorized Participants will be tempted by the risk-free money to arbitrage away even tiny differences.

The fund’s prospectus states that at least 90% of its portfolio will include the securities in the index, and since it is trying to track the index as closely as possible, it is fair to assume that the weighting of every security in the fund will be pretty close to that of the index. Thus, we should have a look at how the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index determines weighting. In short, it is a bit complicated. First, the index is market cap-weighted just like the S&P 500 Index. The higher a given master limited partnership’s market capitalization, the larger its weighting in the index. Also, just like the S&P 500 Index, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index is float-adjusted, so only the partnership units that are available for public trading are included in the calculation.

Where the methodology for the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index differs from the S&P 500 Index is that it has a cap of 12% of the index. Any securities that have a weighting of over 12% whenever the weightings of the index are being recalculated have the excess proportionately distributed to the other securities in the index that have a weighting of less than 12%. This process keeps getting repeated until everything in the index is at 12% or less of the index. As there are thirteen partnerships in the index, it is possible to get all securities at 12% or less weighting.

As mentioned earlier, only master limited partnerships with more than 50% of their cash flow from midstream energy operations, with a median trading volume of $2 million per day, and that have paid distributions for the preceding six-month period are eligible for inclusion.

The index is rebalanced following the above weighting process every quarter on the third Friday of March, June, September, and December. It is not rebalanced outside of these dates, so it is possible for a given company to exceed the 12% weighting on any given date if it outperforms the other companies in the index sufficiently.

It is worth noting that the market capitalizations of the constituent companies are not as of the rebalancing date. Rather, the index designers use the market capitalizations as of the Thursday immediately before the second Friday of March, June, September, and December. Thus, the market capitalizations used for calculating the weightings for the constituents in the index are about a week or so old on the day that the index is rebalanced.

The Alerian MLP ETF only has an 18% annual turnover, so presumably it changes its portfolio whenever the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index undergoes its quarterly rebalancing.

AMLP Peers And Other Options

The Alerian MLP ETF is the only exchange-traded fund on the market that tracks the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index. There is an exchange-traded note available that also tries to track the index, however. This security is the UBS ETRACS Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index ETN Series B (MLPB). As noted earlier in this article, exchange-traded notes can more accurately track master limited partnerships than any exchange-traded fund can because it does not have the drag on their performance from taxes. We can see this pretty easily in the performance of these two securities, as the exchange-traded note has outperformed the Alerian MLP ETF on a total return basis over the past five years:

As a note, the UBS ETRACS Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index ETN Series B is a debt security of UBS (UBS), and as such, it has default risk. UBS is simply paying the holders of this debt security whatever the return of the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index happens to be. This security’s payments are taxed at ordinary income rates for American investors, which could quite possibly be a higher rate than what that same individual would pay on the distributions of the Alerian MLP ETF. This issue could be overcome by putting the note into a retirement account, though. For investors who are looking to get exposure to the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index in their retirement accounts, the UBS ETRACS Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index ETN Series B is probably a better option than the Alerian MLP ETF, if that investor trusts UBS not to default.

There are a few other passive exchange-traded index funds that investors might alternatively choose to gain exposure to master limited partnerships. However, they do not track the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index:

Fund Name

Index Tracked

Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX)

Solactive MLP & Energy Infrastructure Index

Global X MLP ETF (MLPA)

Solactive MLP Infrastructure Index

The indices tracked by these funds are different from the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index, although they might include different companies. The Solactive MLP & Energy Infrastructure Index, for example, consists mostly of midstream energy corporations, not master limited partnerships. Midstream corporations tend to perform somewhat differently from master limited partnerships in the market, and usually have lower yields than master limited partnerships.

As these two funds track different indices, most of the above comments regarding the composition of their portfolios and the methodology used to construct their benchmark indices are not relevant. Thus, the two Global X funds are not directly comparable to the Alerian MLP ETF.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Alerian MLP ETF is one of the more well-known and popular ways for investors to passively track an index of high-yielding midstream master limited partnerships. It has its drawbacks, however, most of which come from U.S. tax laws. Due to these laws, the fund is forced to be structured as a C-corporation and pay taxes on the fund level, which is different from most other passive exchange-traded index funds. This introduces a great deal of tracking error, and this fund consistently underperforms its benchmark index. Investors who are looking to track the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index in a retirement account would probably be better off with an exchange-traded note, but that does require a certain amount of trust in the financial strength of the issuer. Overall, this is not a bad fund as long as one understands its limitations.

This article answers these three main questions about AMLP:

  1. What types of investors is AMLP suitable for?
  2. What are the drawbacks to investing in AMLP?
  3. Does AMLP provide a higher yield than most ETFs?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.


Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Riskified Ltd. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Riskified Ltd. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Wells Fargo: There Are Currently Far Better Deals In The Banking Sector
data.description:
Introduction

Wells Fargo & Company (NYSE: WFC) is one of the major American banks, doing Consumer Banking, Lending, Wealth and Investment Management, as well as Investment Banking. While the company is well known, this analysis will show that there are currently better deals in the banking sector as WFC struggles with growth and an elevated valuation.

Fundamentals

In terms of profitability, WFC looks slightly better than the average. At a net-income margin of 25.88% and ROE of 11.64%, the company slightly beats the industry median of 22.88% and 10.39%, respectively. Nevertheless, Return on Assets is only at 1.03%, slightly below the median of 1.14%.

Concerning revenue growth, Wells Fargo did not perform well at a negative growth rate of -0.45% YoY and a low forward growth rate of 1.87%, far worse than the industry median of over 6%. Nevertheless, EPS diluted growth has exceeded the industry median at 16.01%. Also, ROE growth is positive at 2.71% forward.

When looking at the balance sheet, total assets have slightly grown from 1,929,845.0 million to 1,950,311.0 million. Nevertheless, deposits are down while short-term borrowings and long-term debt are up.

This brings us to valuation. Overall, WFC is more expensive than the industry median according to almost any valuation metric. The P/E ratio at 13.14 is higher than the median of 12.63, the P/S ratio is higher at 3.18, and the P/B ratio is elevated at 1.47 as well. Figure 1 also shows the historical P/E ratio. In the past, the P/E ratio of WFC has already been 3 times at current levels, while it was 3 times lower and 1 time much higher (although this was during 2020 and was likely due to earnings being much lower). This indicates that the stock is, historically speaking, rather expensive. Furthermore, the P/E ratio implies an annualized return of 5.48%, which is solid but not great. Nevertheless, the dividend (Figure 2) is currently high at 2018 levels of around 1.6, while the lowest level was in 2021 at 0.6.

Peer Group Comparison

To gain a better understanding of the quality of WFC, it also makes sense to compare the company to its competitors. In terms of profitability, the company is somewhere in the middle. While JPMorgan Chase & Co. (NYSE:JPM), Bank of America Corporation (NYSE:BAC) and Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) have a higher net income margin of more than 28%, WFC is still better than Citigroup Inc. (NYSE:C) and Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) at only 18.62% and 22.35% respectively. While only JPM and MS have a higher return on assets, WFC also has one of the best ROEs among the big American banks.

In terms of growth, however, WFC is currently the worst company in the just-described peer group. It has not only the worst and only negative revenue growth but also the second-worst diluted EPS growth, with BAC slightly lower at 15.72% in contrast to WFC's 16.01%.

Coming to the valuation, Figure 3 shows the key valuation metrics for the 6 companies. As can be seen, WFC is one of the more expensive companies in terms of the P/E ratio. At the same time, the company's P/S and P/B ratios are somewhere in the middle. Furthermore, Figure 3 also shows the banks' Tier according to the list of globally systemically important banks. This is important as it shows the likelihood of the government helping a company should it ever get into financial trouble. For example, it is highly unlikely that JPM will ever go bankrupt, as it is the only tier 4 bank. Furthermore, JPM is the biggest bank in the world according to market capitalization, making it unlikely to be taken over by another bank. This gives JPM investors more security than shareholders in WFC have due to its lower systemic importance and its smaller size.

All of this looks rather pessimistic for WFC as the company has the worst growth outlook and at best standard profitability, while being highly priced, both from a historical perspective and from the peer group comparison. Furthermore, the tier 1 classification means that WFC only has to hold an additional buffer of 1% of risk-weighted assets, while higher tiers have to hold higher buffers. Normally, this should result in higher growth or at least profitability and further weaken WFC's fundamentals, considering that the company is not exceptional (even weaker than average) in those categories.

Apart from all of this, the WFC does have some significant problems. One of the metrics is Net Interest Income, which is down 6% YoY. Additionally, the management has warned of more losses in this sector as the company de-risks. By doing so, WFC makes the classic mistake of selling its risky assets during poor market conditions, which will make it unable to profit from a potential rebound. In general, the time to sell potentially risky loans would have been before the crash or not at all. Furthermore, it shows poor risk management, as the company took more risk than it is willing to take at the moment, which raises concerns about the overall risk of the stock.

Another factor is credit loss provisions, which are up overall, suggesting more customers are defaulting. While this is again a sign of bad risk management, it might also be an indicator that revenues could continue falling.

Furthermore, the WFC is very reliant on interest-sensitive revenues. This makes a potential recession, with a potentially inverted yield curve, a big risk for the company's earnings.

In total, this shows that WFC has weak risk management. In case of a rally, the company has already de-risked, so that earnings should not rise too much. In case of a further economic downturn, WFC could still underperform due to its interest-sensitive revenues.

General Banking Outlook

When looking at banks, the yield curve (Figure 4) is always very important. As can be seen, the yield curve is not inverted anymore, signaling positive returns for normal banking business, taking the positions for short-term rates and lending them out for long-term ones. Nevertheless, with a recently inverted curve and macroeconomic problems, a recession is still on the table, which could be rather bad for finance stocks, as giving out loans gets more risky. While, at least for now, financial conditions seem to stay neutral, as can be seen in Figure 5, showing the credit spread between US Aaa and Baa corporate bonds, which is on the rise but not yet high, the risk of a recession should not be underestimated. All of this culminates in a neutral to bearish outlook for banking stocks.

Investment Thesis

So what does this whole analysis imply? First of all, it shows that WFC is currently not one of the best banking stocks to own due to it having the worst growth, solid to bad profitability, low systemic importance, and comparatively high prices. For investors, this means that WFC is a clear sell. Furthermore, I would not short the company without having a long position in other banking stocks. A pairs trade could be interesting by shorting WFC and going long on either a basket of banks or a single stock. Here, I would look for stocks that dominate in one given category. JPM, for example, does have the highest valuation but also the best fundamentals in general. In contrast, C is the cheapest stock in all categories, and GS has a very high diluted EPS growth of over 65%. Together, those 3 companies could make a very compelling basket against WFC.

Conclusion

In total, WFC is not one of the greatest finance stocks at the current moment across categories. At the same time, the outlook for banks is very uncertain and could become more bearish in the future. While I would not risk just shorting WFC, the company is still a sell, which investors can profit from by trading a basket of other stocks on the long side against a possible short in WFC.

data.title: Polestar: Keep Selling Until Equity Raised
data.description:
Before the opening bell on Monday, we received some limited first quarter results from Polestar (NASDAQ:PSNY). The electric vehicle maker has been one of the most disappointing names in the space in recent years. The company has failed to grow its unit sales as fast as hoped, leading to large losses and cash burn. While the company is starting to make some progress on its turnaround efforts, I remain a bit bearish as the uphill battle has a long way to go.

Previous coverage of the name

My most recent article on the name came back in January, after which the company had reported some Q3 figures. As a reminder, when Polestar originally went public the goal was for 65,000 deliveries in 2022, then 124,000 in 2023, followed by 225,000 in 2024 and 290,000 in 2025. The company ended up cutting guidance for 2025 to about 155,000 units, but after delivering less than 45,000 last year, the new plan is to head towards about 110,000 annual sales by 2027.

While shares have bounced about 1% or so since I last looked at the stock, slightly outpacing the S&P's small loss, those gains only have recovered a drop in the bucket from the roughly $13 price point at which I first wrote about Polestar. At least for now, however, shares are well off their all-time public trading low of 61 cents seen last year.

A look at Q1 results

For the quarter, Polestar delivered a little over 12,300 cars. This was up 76% over the prior year period, but obviously nowhere near where the company thought it would be just a year or two ago. In fact, the number is just a couple hundred units above where it was in Q1 2023. New models drove most of the growth, as the Polestar 3 and Polestar 4 have become more available globally, joining sales of the Polestar 2. Later this year, the company is set to launch the Polestar 5.

With a favorable shift in product mix, the company saw revenues surge by more than 84% to $608 million. This did miss street estimates by almost $70 million, as expectations called for almost 96% growth. As the newer models have started to ramp a bit, and featured more impressive margins, the company reported an overall gross margin number of 6.8% for Q1, compared to negative 7.7% in the year ago period. The need for competitive pricing did negatively impact margins a little, however.

The overall loss was nearly $200 million, but it did improve almost a third from the year ago period. There are a number of one-time items in the income statement that impacted things as well, given the company's complex financial structure and some joint ventures. Last week, Polestar finally released its 2024 annual report, which gave investors a more complete view of prior results.

The balance sheet remains troubled

The company hasn't released its full set of Q1 financial statements yet. At the end of last year, cash was $740 million but interest bearing liabilities stood at more than $4 billion, while the working capital deficit stood at more than $2.4 billion. At the end of 2024, cash minus interest bearing liabilities was negative $3.346 billion, an increase of about $525 million over the prior year period.

Management did give us some color on the conference call regarding its current situation. Total cash ended Q1 at around $732 million, while the total amount of debt was $4.8 billion, nearing their $5.5 billion debt covenant. While they are looking to reduce cash burn, Polestar is also looking to raise fresh capital through equity, whether it come from main owner Geely or other outside investors.

Valuation is heavily pressured

As I've discussed in many recent articles regarding Polestar, shares have come crashing down in the past few years. As a result, the current valuation is the lowest in the electric vehicle space, but a little above traditional US automakers like Ford (F) and General Motors (GM) that go for about 0.25 times their expected 2025 sales. Polestar finished Monday trading at about 0.56 times its expected revenues for this year, but analyst estimates may come down after the Q1 top line miss.

As the chart below shows, Polestar trades at a price to sales number that's less than its closest counterparts, the Chinese EV companies NIO (NIO), XPeng (XPEV), and BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF). The only name currently cheaper is Zeekr (ZK). Polestar goes for just a fraction of the valuation of names like Rivian (RIVN), VinFast (VFS), and Lucid (LCID). EV giant Tesla (TSLA) trades at a near double-digit multiple on its price to expected sales, a couple dozen times the multiple of Polestar, so it gets excluded below because it would really skew the chart.

When it comes to street analysts, the picture is a bit mixed. Going into Monday's report, there were three sell ratings against plus two holds. The average price target of $1.01 implied high single digit percentage downside from Monday's close. However, that valuation figure has been slashed from over $3.25 a year ago, and it was in the low double digits in late 2022 after Polestar completed its SPAC merger.

Final thoughts and recommendations

While Polestar's report on Monday did show some encouraging signs, the company is still in a very troubling place right now. Deliveries are not even close to where projections were initially, and the balance sheet needs a significant amount of help. It was nice to see gross margins finally start to rebound, but this is not a company that can survive when selling low to mid teens thousands of vehicles a quarter.

Until a much needed equity infusion comes, I am continuing to rate this stock as a sell. Polestar badly needs to raise this capital to help its balance sheet, but the stock is down more than 90% already from when it completed its SPAC merger. There is also the possible negative catalyst of a reverse split to help with exchange listing requirements if the stock is it falls under a dollar. Once the balance sheet gets some help and we see if deliveries can really start to grow with decent margins, then I will look at my rating again.

data.title: Ethereum May Be Starting A New Secular Bull Phase
data.description:
I've been bullish on Bitcoin, and that's been validated by market action. After hitting a new all-time high around USD 108k, BTC-USD has consolidated in a wide ranging sideways pattern for about 6 months, since mid-December.

The existing trend channel has held well, and the pattern remains bullish. There's an argument for a continuation of the sideways action for some time, but there's probably a better argument for a renewed breakout and a continuation of the bull trend towards an initial target of 185k, and then on to 300k.

Opportunity Knocks

I've largely ignored the "crypto" craze and focused on Bitcoin as a new store of value and medium of exchange for the the emergent Intelligence Economy. But about a month ago, after reviewing the charts of the larger cryptocurrencies, I sent a note to subscribers of the BullBear Market Report about Ethereum (ETH-USD). I noticed some things on the charts that looked a lot like opportunity, and I was personally, at that moment, diving into the creation of a token for a new application that I'm building, Intellipedia.AI. I told my subscribers that it would be worthwhile to rotate out of some portion of Bitcoin holdings into Ethereum. That analysis has also been validated by market action.

The first thing I noticed was that the relative performance chart of Ethereum/Bitcoin close to its lowest point ever. And at the same time I saw that the last time it was in this zone, it was a tremendous relative performance play.

So I had a look at the ETH chart:

...and I noticed that whenever weekly RSI was down at these level, huge rallies have always followed. The rally from the 2020 low was 3,300%.

We can also see that since 2021, after that huge rally, ETH has essentially moved sideways in a wide-ranging correction. Four years of consolidative correction is a long time. Generally, the longer the correction, the more explosive and protracted the rally that follows. This looks to be a setup for the 3rd wave of the Major 5th bull impulse move for Ethereum. That's a powerful wave position, and in the cryptocurrency world, 5th waves are usually runaway bull rampages.

Here's a composite chart of the top 10 altcoins (excluding Bitcoin):

In many ways, this group is positioned like the techs were in the wake of the 2000-2003 dotcom crash. There was inflated hype, wild speculation, a crash and then out of the ashes, the real players emerged.

My take is that Bitcoin and Ethereum are among the real players that will separate from the ashes and go on to be the crypto equivalents of AAPL and GOOG.

Since drafting this article, ETH-USD has continued its rally, now about 100% off it's low:

My projection for this rally is to about USD $10,000.

Once the resistance at 4K is taken out, we shouldn't see much of a correction until weekly RSI gets into its resistance zone, at which point we should look for a bear divergence in between price and the indicator, and consider banking some profit, or hedging for a substantive pullback.

Risks to this analysis include:

  • changes in macro policy
  • Fed tightening of monetary policy
  • BTC price drop below 89-95k support
  • ETH price drop below USD 2000

In my opinion, it's not too late to take a position. If you already have some BTC or other cryptocurrency, swapping some of it for ETH is a great play right now.

data.title: Gold's Basel III Boost: Why KBWB Could Shine In 2025
data.description:
Gold shines in the eyes of many market participants. The precious metal soared to $3,500 per Troy ounce earlier this year, and while it has pulled back, traders, investors, and now regulators are taking a shine to this safe-haven asset.

A recent Gallup poll revealed that Americans view gold as a better long-term investment than stocks, second only to real estate (which is rarely not the top spot in that survey). Moreover, central banks have been major net gold buyers in the last decade-plus, which provides a fundamental, often price-insensitive demand source.

Amid what’s clearly a bull market in sentiment and price action, global lawmakers are now entering the scene.

Effective July 1, Basel III “Endgame” rules will reportedly reclassify gold as a Tier 1 High-Quality Liquid Asset, equivalent to cash or Treasurys. That’s significant news because it means US banks will be able to consider gold holdings at 100% of market value toward core capital reserves. Previously, gold was a Tier 3 asset, which meant it had to be discounted by 50% on bank balance sheets.

The upgrade comes at a key time for large and mid-sized US financial institutions—the Q1 earnings season featured increases in loan-loss provisions. With gold now counting more toward Tier 1 capital, bank assets could now be used for more pro-growth initiatives, such as capital markets activities or doling out loans to would-be borrowers.

Amid these changes, I remain bullish on the Invesco KBW Bank ETF (NASDAQ:KBWB). Shares are flat since my December 2024 analysis, outperforming the S&P 500 by about three percentage points. I will note that the fund remains a compelling value, and there are firm signs of a technical bottom.

Gold Catching Investors' Eyes

Heavy Central Bank Gold Buying

According to the issuer, KBWB is based on the KBW Nasdaq Bank Index. The ETF will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in the securities that comprise the underlying index. Using a modified-market cap-weighted index of companies primarily engaged in US banking activities, the fund is composed of large national US money centers, regional banks, and thrift institutions that are publicly traded in the US. KBWB is rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly.

Despite flat returns so far in 2025, KBWB’s assets under management has swelled from $2.8 billion last December to $3.8 billion today. Its annual expenses ratio is low to moderate at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is about a full percentage point above that of the S&P 500 at 2.39% as of May 13, 2025.

Share-price momentum remains strong—its ETF Grade in that category is an “A,” according to Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. KBWB can be a risky product, however, and investors endured intense volatility in the sessions after Liberation Day, but those who held the fund were rewarded in the weeks following the April 7 intraday bottom. Finally, the ETF sports solid liquidity metrics—its average daily volume is high at above 2.2 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is low at just three basis points, per Invesco.

For an update on the portfolio, the 2-star ETF by Morningstar plots on the far-left side of the style box, indicating its high weight to the value style. Forty-four percent of the allocation is considered in the “SMID” cap space, which adds to its cyclical exposure. Still, the big banks—think JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Wells Fargo (WFC), Morgan Stanley (MS), Goldman Sachs (GS), and Bank of America (BAC)--are the real drivers of KBWB price action.

The ETF sports a very low 11.0x price-to-earnings ratio, while its long-term EPS growth rate is robust at 11.6%, resulting in a compelling PEG ratio under 1x.

KBWB: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

I expect KBWB to feature modest volatility through the end of the first half of 2025. Bank earnings are done, and market-wide implied volatility gauges have retreated substantially from their April zeniths. From a longer-term perspective, I’m encouraged to see that the fund’s dividend payout has increased since 2020. That tells me that banks are rewarding shareholders, despite bouts of macro volatility in the last five years.

Overall, the allocation is somewhat concentrated—there's more than 60% invested in the top 10 stocks.

KBWB: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal perspective, May and June tend to produce lukewarm returns when scanning price data over the past 10 years. July is among the best months of the year, so perhaps we see a rally in advance of and through bank earnings season, which begins in mid-July.

KBWB: Muted Q2 Returns Historically

The Technical Take

With positive macro news regarding the Basel III rules and given KBWB’s attractive valuation, the technical situation is mixed, but broadly encouraging. Notice in the chart below that shares held key long-term support during the post-Liberation Day tumble. The low was $51, and the bank ETF trades above $66 today. This whippy price action comes as the long-term 200-day moving average remains trending higher—that tells me the bulls control the primary trend.

Also, take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph—it's now above 70, helping to confirm the nearly 30% rally off the April nadir. I see resistance at a pair of spots. The first is the $71 to $73 zone, which was the double top from late 2024 and this past February. The second is the all-time high, which dates back to January 2022. There is a downside price gap near $57, so that should act as support if the key $60 mark does not hold.

KBWB: Shares Back Above the 200dma, $72 Resistance, Strong RSI Trends

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on KBWB. The apparent news that gold will be counted as a Tier 1, High-Quality Liquid Asset is a net positive for banks. It allows them to embark on higher-ROI projects while maintaining proper capital ratios. With a low valuation and decent chart, I expect gains over the balance of the year.

data.title: Pinterest: Unsustainable Growth And User Backlash Signal Danger Ahead
data.description:
The Setup

Pinterest (NYSE:PINS) recently sold off from ~$40/share in February to the mid-20s in April because of macro and tariff concerns. The stock recovered some of its losses leading up to earnings, and subsequently jumped to $32/share after Q1 earnings were announced on May 8th. Shares stabilized in the $29 to $30 range on May 9th before rallying to ~$33/share on May 12th after the US and China announced a 90-day tariff reduction.

The initial PINS sell-off seems warranted because advertising dollars are highly macro-sensitive. Also, tariffs are problematic because they impact Pinterest's e-commerce advertisers. E-commerce companies will theoretically advertise less on Pinterest because of tariffs, as selling products sourced from China may no longer be profitable. However, tariff concerns were brushed aside during the latest earnings call when Julia Donnelly, Pinterest’s CFO, said, “There are small pockets of spend that have been impacted by tariffs in recent weeks.”

Although the market liked Pinterest’s recent earnings and the tariff announcement, I am more bearish about the company’s long-term outlook. In my view, the narrative presented by management is overly optimistic.

Pinterest’s Revenue Growth Isn’t Sustainable

Management’s 3–5-year revenue growth targets were set at mid-high teens during Pinterest’s 2023 Investor Day. Sell-side estimates for revenue growth remain in the mid-teens for 2025, 2026, and 2027, according to CapIQ.

However, most revenue growth is driven by Pinterest’s US and Canada (UCAN) average revenue per user (ARPU) growth.

ARPU growth has been primarily driven by ad load growth, an unsustainable source of growth because Pinterest can only show users a finite number of ads. Signs of excessive ad load are already showing. ARPU was $6.54 in Q1’25 for UCAN – 8% YoY growth relative to $6.05 in Q1’24, a substantial decrease from the 13.9% YoY UCAN ARPU growth in 2024 (UCAN ARPU growth is calculated in this Excel file using data from Pinterest's 10-K).

Additionally, Reddit is flooded with complaints about high ad load. To compare ad complaints over time, I used a Google account with no search history and used the query “Pinterest site:reddit.com.” The date range was limited to one-year periods. Then, I looked at the first 10 search results and determined the percentage with complaints about too many ads.

Users are not happy with Pinterest’s direction. Notably, the first 10 search results for other platforms (Facebook, Snapchat, etc.) primarily focused on establishing an online presence on those platforms.

My view of sustainable US revenue growth aligns more closely with US user growth. US ARPU growth slowing down could take 5pps or more off annual revenue growth and cause Pinterest to miss sell-side estimates. My view of US user growth is pessimistic because users are unhappy with the increase in ad load (as shown in the chart above). Users are also dissatisfied with low-quality AI content and worsening search functionality, which I will explain in more detail later.

Ad Pricing is at Risk

Pinterest has significantly increased the volume of ads on its platform. Ad impressions were up 49% YoY, but ad prices were down 22% YoY. Management claimed that most of the negative price impact was due to a mix shift resulting from international growth. When asked about UCAN ad pricing, though, Pinterest’s CFO was vague in her response. Julia Donnelly said, “If you were to look at kind of UCAN impression growth and pricing alone, it would tell a very different story, and the trends are far less pronounced because the primary driver on a global level really is this mix shift impact due to international growth.” So, while UCAN ad prices aren’t down by >20% per year, the vagueness of Julia’s response indicates that prices are flat to down in my opinion.

Going forward, ad pricing is likely to be pressured as Pinterest continues to increase its ad load. After all, there is a finite number of relevant ads that Pinterest can display to users.

Rebuttal to Bullish Arguments

Bullish investors will likely counter by claiming that the Rest of the World's growth will compensate for potentially declining US ARPU growth. However, much of Pinterest’s international revenue growth is driven by ad load growth, so international revenue growth is likely not sustainable at current levels. Additionally, international growth is of relatively low value and potentially margin-dilutive, as Pinterest needs to allocate engineering resources to service other countries while generating very little revenue. GDP per capita is much lower in Pinterest’s Rest of the World countries, so advertising space is less valuable.

The bulls would also argue that Pinterest is utilizing AI to target ads more effectively at users. This argument appears weak because users are dissatisfied with the volume of ads on Pinterest. It doesn’t matter if ads are slightly more relevant when users want a higher ratio of user-generated content, but all they see are ads. They will eventually vote with their feet.

The User Experience Flywheel is Exaggerated

Management argues that ads are additive to the user experience. Therefore, more ads lead to a better user experience, which in turn leads to increased engagement, resulting in more advertisers, and ultimately, more engaging ads.

Pinterest’s CEO argues that ads are additive because Pinterest users have “commercial intent.” However, the data indicates that Pinterest users have little commercial intent. As noted earlier, users are unhappy with the increased ad load. Pinterest’s average cost per click is also significantly lower than that of its competitors, further suggesting a lack of "commercial intent."

Additionally, other parts of the flywheel are questionable. It’s a stretch to say that more advertisers lead to more effective and engaging ads in my opinion. More advertisers may mean that Pinterest can show the user more relevant ads, but this impact is likely minimal.

The User Experience Is Declining, so the “Flywheel” Could be in Reverse

Ads

As mentioned earlier, users are very unhappy with the increased ad load. Even employees dislike the focus on ads.

Low-Quality AI Content

Pinterest’s user base is extremely unhappy with AI content flooding their feeds. To approximate the increase in AI complaints over time, I used the same “Pinterest site:reddit.com” query and looked at the first 10 posts (note: multiple posts complained about ads and AI simultaneously).

Pinterest is aware of the AI problem and has taken steps to combat it. However, it seems that they’ve been largely unsuccessful because their software, which automatically detects AI-generated content, has been prone to false positives and is suspending legitimate accounts.

The AI problem will worsen over time as people become more familiar with AI image tools. AI could pose an existential risk to Pinterest because Pinterest users, who are often seeking inspiration, are strongly opposed to AI-generated content.

Worsening Search Functionality

To approximate the increase in search functionality complaints over time, I used the same “Pinterest site:reddit.com” query and looked at the first 10 posts (note: multiple posts complained about ads, AI, and search functionality simultaneously).

An example of a user complaint is below.

Where could I be wrong?

The primary drivers of Pinterest’s intrinsic value are ARPU and user growth. If management can leverage AI to expand ARPU growth and incorporate features that enhance the user experience, Pinterest could outperform my expectations for ARPU growth and user growth. It’s also possible that management figures out how to deliver sustained ARPU and MAU growth in Pinterest’s international markets, resulting in a larger contribution to overall revenue growth.

Also, Pinterest's average cost per click and cost per thousand impressions are well below its competitors. If management figures out how to close this gap, the stock could potentially be undervalued at current levels. However, history has shown that Pinterest users are far less valuable than users on other platforms.

Valuation

Revenue Model

I break revenue growth into ARPU and user growth for each geography. I have ARPU growth slowing to a more sustainable rate of 5% for Europe and the US, whereas ARPU and user growth stay elevated in the rest of the world. I model relatively stable user growth in the US and Europe. This is likely an optimistic assumption given user experience issues.

EBIT Model

I assume that G&A stays at $488 million (average of 2023 and 2024) to model in operating leverage. Additionally, I assume a slight decrease in R&D as a percent of revenue because international expansion will require more R&D resources, but Pinterest is heavily investing in AI personnel. There's room for investment in AI personnel to moderate over time. I assume S&M and COGS are more variable.

Relative Valuation

Pure-play comparables for Pinterest don't exist. Pinterest is a combination of a social, search, and e-commerce business. Meta and Google are of much higher quality than Pinterest, but Snapchat and Etsy are lower quality, so the average multiples indicate where Pinterest should trade.

Pinterest looks fairly valued using EV/Sales multiples but overvalued using EV/EBIT multiples. Using the comps' average EV/Sales multiple might overvalue Pinterest because Meta pulls the peer group average up. As a result, my view is that $14.73-$30.07 is a fair valuation range for Pinterest (which excludes the Comps Average EV/Sales Multiple scenario).

Conclusion

Given the risks involved, Pinterest looks expensive at 5.3x EV/Sales (TTM). ARPU growth is likely unsustainable and has already weakened. User experience complaints are also everywhere, but have yet to appear in MAU data. Excessive ads and AI content will likely only get worse as time progresses - therefore, I'm bearish on Pinterest and believe it's worth closer to the bottom end of my $14.73-$30.07 valuation range.

data.title: Trinity Industries: A Good Company In A Challenging Environment
data.description:
Investment thesis

The Seeking Alpha summary page for Trinity Industries, Inc. (NYSE:TRN) includes a bright red banner warning: “TRN is at high risk of performing badly”.

I argue in this article that investors should check out the warning, but it should be just one of several factors to consider in making a buy or sell decision. In my examination of the warning, I found it was of limited value.

As for Trinity’s prospects and valuation, both are good, but I am concerned about challenges from tariffs and a potential economic slowdown. I rate it a Hold.

About Trinity

It is a major player among railcar lessors and manufacturers in North America. Founded in 1933, it operates under the tradenames of TrinityRail, RSI Logistics, and Holden America.

It operates through two segments, Railcar Leasing and Services Group, and Rail Products Group. The former provides what it calls in the 10-K for 2024, “comprehensive railcar industry services”. The main business of this group is leasing freight and tank railcars.

The latter, Rail Products Group, manufactures freight and tank railcars for the North American market. It also has a parts and components business and it holds patents applicable to manufacturing railcars and components.

At the close on May 13, shares traded at $26.79 and it had a market cap of $2.16 billion.

Competition and competitive advantages

According to the 10-K it faces “aggressive competition”, on the basis of performance, technological innovation, quality, reliability of delivery, customer service, and more. I note that this list does not include price, although that might be expected in a highly competitive market.

Competitors include The Greenbrier Companies, Inc. (GBX), GATX Corporation (GATX) (which is also a customer for Trinity-manufactured railcars), and Union Tank Car Company, a subsidiary of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B).

Despite the competition, the company said in its 10-K, “We believe we are a leader in the rail industry digital transformation by providing digital and terminal management services designed to increase the efficiency and visibility of the supply chain, while leveraging data, insights, and analytics to make decisions that improve operations and reduce costs.”

To assess its competitiveness, we will compare its net income margin and return on equity with those of Greenbrier and GATX. First, net margins:

The standout in this group is obviously GATX, while Greenbrier and Trinity are more than 10 points behind; as the chart legend shows, Trinity is about a point below Greenbrier.

When it comes to returns on equity or ROE, the positions are reversed, with Greenbrier and Trinity leading GATX:

Based on this information, I would assess Trinity as having a narrow to medium moat.

First quarter results

Trinity released its Q1-2025 earnings report on May 1, 2025. These are the key points:

  • Quarterly revenue of $585 million, down from $809.6 million in Q1-2024.
  • Net income of $24.0 million, down from $28.0 million in 2024.
  • Diluted EPS GAAP of $0.29, down from $0.33 in 2024.

Here’s more detailed information, from the earnings report:

The poorer results mainly stem from a serious dip in Rail Products Group revenue. In Q1-2025 it had revenues of $420.5 million, down from $667.4 million in Q1-2024. On the Q1 earnings call, President and CEO Jean Savage said,

“Our results in this segment reflect the current operating environment. We delivered 3,060 new railcars in the quarter and received orders for 695 railcars, evidence of the delayed investment decisions I have previously acknowledged and the lumpiness of orders quarter-to-quarter. As a result, quarterly revenue was down due to lower deliveries.”

Delayed investment decisions: That’s Trinity’s challenge at the moment, and it could be deep-seated. As Matt Watters and Shubham Singhal noted in an April 23, 2025 article for the consulting firm, McKinsey, “The uncertainty about the tariffs and possible retaliations or negotiations have created one of the greatest levels of peacetime uncertainty for US businesses.”

As a result, I don’t believe delayed investment decisions will reverse in the near future, which means continued challenges for Trinity’s top and bottom lines.

On the other hand, there are several positives about Trinity’s situation. First, fleet utilization is high, at just under 97.0%, with management saying on the earnings call that the North American railcar fleet is in balance. In this state, there is no excess capacity and pricing is rational.

Second, it has been able to push up its lease rates and still enjoy high renewal levels. According to management, “Lease rates are driven by many factors, but the most important is a balanced fleet.”

Third, it had a backlog of $1.9 billion at the end of the first quarter. That’s reassuring for a company that had revenue of $3.079 billion last year.

The dividend

While existing shareholders are no doubt discouraged by the sagging share price, new buyers at least have the consolation of a rising dividend. Indeed, the price has fallen far enough for the dividend yield to reach 4.53% at the close on May 13, when the price was $26.79.

The dollar amount is $1.20 per year and it has grown by an average of 10.01% per year over the past five years. It has increased for 14 consecutive years.

The warning

Returning to the Seeking Alpha system warning about Trinity “performing badly”, it lists two characteristics associated with poor future stock performance. First, declining growth, and second decelerating momentum. What’s more, growth has declined enough to generate a Sell rating from the Quant system.

Let’s review its revenue, net income, and basic EPS over the past five years:

Yes, revenue has declined over the past three quarters, but as the chart illustrates, that’s after 11 consecutive quarters of increases.

Net income has dipped, slightly over the past two quarters, but the second dip is relatively small. EPS fell from $187.4 million in Q3-2024 to $157.1 million in Q4-2024, and to $156.8 million in Q1-2025. So, really, there has been only one problematic quarter in the past nine quarters.

Basic EPS basically reflects what has happened with net income over the past three quarters, but the drop was more severe in Q3-2024. Again, we have one bad quarter in the past nine quarters.

Apparently, then, what the company has done or not done is not likely responsible for the warning. That leaves momentum; has it been a problem? Well, it was, and I stress the past tense:

As this table from the Seeking Alpha system shows, there is a clear distinction between what has happened in the past week and month, and what has happened over longer periods.

It appears the data in this table reflect the market downdraft that occurred as tariffs were being discussed, imposed, retracted, and imposed again. It further suggests that concern about tariffs and a potential recession peaked, and then settled back down again.

Finally, note that Trinity has no direct influence over momentum, it emerges out of the actions of millions of individual investors and thousands of firms including the biggest institutional investors.

How much weight should we give this warning? Investors considering buying or selling the stock should certainly read it and assess it. But, I believe it should be just one part of thorough due diligence.

Trinity’s growth prospects

This slide from the Q1-2025 presentation shows management’s guidance for full-year 2025:

Turning to the specifics, it forecast about 35,000 railcar deliveries in the Q4-2025 results; it has now cut that to 28,000-33,000 deliveries. At the midpoint of 30,500 cars, that’s reduction of about 12.86% one quarter later.

Net fleet investment remains the same at $300-$400 million.

Operating and administrative capital expenditures estimates remain the same from Q4: $45 million - $55 million.

Q1’s forecast of EPS ranging from $1.40 - $1.60 is down from Q4’s forecast of $1.50 - $1.80. Using the two midpoints, $1.50 and $1.65, the company is guiding 10% lower now than it did three months earlier. Presumably, that reflects mainly the dip in railcar deliveries.

From another perspective, Railcar Leasing and Services is relatively immune because most of its leases are long term and thus less affected by short-term uncertainty. However, economic uncertainty is slowing orders for new railcars, and pulling down segment revenues.

Therefore, I think it is a reasonable possibility that Trinity’s earnings could go even lower.

According to a report from TD Cowen and cited in Yahoo!Finance, shipping volumes at U.S. ports are down significantly. The west coast has been hit hardest, with volume down 30%. On the east coast, volume has slipped 12%.

The decline is mostly driven by retail and apparel firms temporarily ending their shipments of non-essential goods from China. Some companies did move freight/inventory in the first quarter, and that will exacerbate the situation in the second quarter and beyond.

The problem for Trinity, of course, is that when there is no shipping from China, there is reduced need for railcars to move containers and other freight within the U.S. And when railcars aren’t moving they are not wearing out or as in demand.

Such thinking may have influenced the normalized EPS estimates of Wall Street analysts, who see 2025’s result near the bottom of the company’s forecast range:

I think there is little doubt that 2025 will be a challenging year for Trinity, but by how much and for long are questions we are unable to answer now.

Valuation

The share price peaked at $39.83 in January 2025, about the time when concern about tariffs began to take hold. As the one-year chart shows, it has had many more down days than up days in recent months:

A quick sidenote: While the 52-week high is listed as $39.83, the Historical Prices (Charting page) shows the high as $39.45. This discrepancy appears to originate with the raw data provided to Seeking Alpha.

With the price down, it earns better valuation grades. Notably, its P/E ratios are below those of the Industrials median:

Given the undervaluation, drooping earnings for this year, and the fact Trinity is a well-managed company, I’m going to assume the price will be about the same in 12 months. That means, of course, some ups and downs, but at the end of 12 months to be near where it is now.

Given this situation, I rate Trinity a Hold. There have been no other Seeking Alpha analyst ratings in the past 90 days. The Quant system has had a Sell rating since April 28; previously it had rated it a Hold.

Even the usually bullish Wall Street analysts have a Hold rating, although they expect the price to rise by 9.91% over the coming year. Along with that go one Down revision and no Up revisions.

Risk factors

The biggest current risk to Trinity is the tariff situation, and the associated uncertainty. With the market for rail shipments already declining because of the tariff war with China, demand for new railcars will continue to lag.

The warning about the risk of performing badly may become reality, although I think the concern is somewhat overblown. As we saw, it mostly pertains to momentum, and that has turned in the past month and week.

In this article, I make several assumptions about the future, as do the company and Wall Street analysts. Those assumptions may prove to be incorrect if there continue to be abrupt changes in the political and economic environment.

Trinity operates in a world of aggressive competition, and failure to make technological progress, to use an appropriate business model, or to let its costs get too high could lead to loss of market share.

In the 10-K, it points to the fact its customers operate in cyclical markets. In turn, that means unpredictable demand and volatility. In particular, if markets for energy products, agricultural products, and consumer goods all slowed at the same time, there could be serious repercussions for the company.

Conclusion

I believe Trinity Industries is a strong, well-managed company that should reward shareholders in coming years. At the moment, though, it faces a couple of challenges. First, the slipping share price has triggered a warning to investors, which I consider overblown. It is also challenged by tariffs and the uncertainty they generate.

While it is a good candidate for growth investors, it may take several quarters or more to see it get back on the growth path. I rate Trinity a Hold.
